docs.title: Home
docs.text:
WebSite that tries to list, with descriptiors, music examples, and links, all the objects, externals, and libraries available for PureData.

Random Objects Random Videos

docs.title: Deken: Package Management for Pure Data Externals
docs.text:
Deken is the easiest way to download and install external objects in Pure Data (Pd).

Open Deken from Pd’s menu:

  • Pd 0.56 and later: Tools → Find Externals.
  • Pd earlier versions: Help → Find Externals.

The video below shows how to install giga.rev~ using Deken.

docs.title: pd.cmake
docs.text: pd.cmake is a collection of CMake scripts designed to simplify the process of creating build projects for Pure Data (Pd) externals. It provides a unified, cross-platform way to generate build files (Makefiles, Xcode projects, Visual Studio solutions, etc.) for Pd externals using CMake.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
• Automates the setup of CMake projects for Pd external development.
  • Ensures compatibility across multiple operating systems and IDEs.
  • Reduces boilerplate by providing reusable CMake modules specific to Pd.

docs.title: Use caseVideos
docs.text:
Instead of manually writing platform-specific build scripts, developers can use pd.cmake to quickly generate the necessary build environment for compiling Pd externals.

Contributors
  • Download here.
  • Developed by Pierre Guiellot, Charles K. Neimog, and others.
  • Report Bugs here!
  • Available for .

docs.title: pd-lib-builder
docs.text: Makefile.pdlibbuilder is a helper Makefile designed to streamline the building of Pure Data (Pd) external libraries.

docs.title: Key points
docs.text:
• Created by Katja Vetter (2015) and released to the public domain.
  • Developed further as a Pd community project.
  • Inspired by Hans Christoph Steiner’s Makefile Template and Stephan Beal’s ShakeNMake.
  • Requires GNU make ≥ 3.81.

docs.title: PurposeVideos
docs.text:
• Provides a standardized and portable way to compile Pd externals.
  • Handles platform differences (Linux, macOS, Windows) automatically.
  • Reduces complexity compared to writing custom Makefiles.

Use msys2 on Windows

On windows it is easiear to use msys2 to build objects with pd-lib-builder. You can install msys2 running winget install msys2.msys2 or check https://www.msys2.org/.

Contributors
  • Download here.
  • Developed by Pierre Guiellot, Charles K. Neimog, and others.
  • Report Bugs here!
  • Available for .

docs.title: Objects
docs.text:
In this section we list all the objects submit for the project. Note that, if there is some great object is missing, please submit it using Submit form. It is very simple and easy. If you did some piece, publish article or recorded a Video for some object and it is not listed here also submit it.

AI Classification

These libraries are extensive, and the first step involves AI-based classification. If you notice an error, please click "Submit" enter the object name, and update its description and classification.

Random Objects

docs.title: Additive
docs.text:
• cavoc27~ The cavoc27~ object generates audio spectra using a 27-rule cellular automaton.
  • oscbank~ The oscbank~ object generates complex audio signals by combining multiple sine~ oscillators, allowing users to define a list of partials, amplitudes, and phases for each.
  • enrich~ The enrich~ object performs additive synthesis by generating an oscillator bank.
  • blip~ blip~ is a band-limited oscillator that generates waveforms by summing cosines using Discrete-Summation Formulae (DSF).
  • pileup~ The pileup~ object performs spectral persistence by maintaining amplitude and phase information in frequency bins.
  • cavoc~ The cavoc~ object generates audio spectra using an 8-rule cellular automaton.

docs.title: blip~
docs.text:
blip~ is a band-limited oscillator that generates waveforms by summing cosines using Discrete-Summation Formulae (DSF). It allows precise control over the fundamental frequency, number of harmonics, spectral multiplier, and lowest harmonic, enabling the creation of various timbres. It also supports phase modulation and hard/soft sync, with optional MIDI pitch input for frequency.

AI Generated

This content was generated with the assistance of AI. If you notice any errors, please report them or submit a fix using Submit. Check the prompt used here.

  • Download via Deken.

    Open Pd and go to ToolsFind Externals. Search for else and install it. Then create an object with declare -lib else -path else. Finally, use blip~ or any other object from else.

  • Library developed mainly by Alexandre Torres Porres.
  • Report Bugs/Errors here!
  • Available for .
Comments Contributors

docs.title: cavoc27~
docs.text:
The cavoc27~ object generates audio spectra using a 27-rule cellular automaton. It can interpolate between spectra, freeze the current spectrum, and transpose its output, especially when using its internal oscillator bank for resynthesis. Additionally, cavoc27~ can capture external audio spectra to use as a tuning source, allowing for dynamic interaction with other audio signals.

AI Generated

This content was generated with the assistance of AI. If you notice any errors, please report them or submit a fix using Submit. Check the prompt used here.

  • Download via Deken.

    Open Pd and go to ToolsFind Externals. Search for fftease and install it. Then create an object with declare -lib fftease -path fftease. Finally, use cavoc27~ or any other object from fftease.

  • Library developed mainly by Eric Lyon.
  • Report Bugs/Errors here!
  • Available for .
Articles

  • Pd and Audio Programming in the 21st Century by Eric Lyon

    Link

Comments Contributors

docs.title: cavoc~
docs.text:
The cavoc~ object generates audio spectra using an 8-rule cellular automaton. It can be triggered by an internal clock or external bangs, allowing for dynamic control over spectral density, frequency range, and phase transposition. This object is ideal for creating evolving and complex timbres through algorithmic synthesis.

AI Generated

docs.title: enrich~
docs.text:
The enrich~ object performs additive synthesis by generating an oscillator bank. It can resynthesize signals, allowing for the addition of harmonics and manipulation of frequency ranges. Users can draw directly into an array to define arbitrary waveforms for synthesis.

docs.title: oscbank~
docs.text:
The oscbank~ object generates complex audio signals by combining multiple sine~ oscillators, allowing users to define a list of partials, amplitudes, and phases for each. It takes a frequency multiplier as its main input, serving as the fundamental frequency for the entire bank. Additionally, it supports multichannel output, where each individual oscillator is routed to a separate channel.

AI Generated

This content was generated with the assistance of AI. If you notice any errors, please report them or submit a fix using Submit. Check the prompt used here.

  • Download via Deken.

    Open Pd and go to ToolsFind Externals. Search for else and install it. Then create an object with declare -lib else -path else. Finally, use oscbank~ or any other object from else.

docs.title: Algorithmic
docs.text: • loop The loop object in Pure Data provides a flexible iteration mechanism, functioning as a counter that can increment or decrement through a specified range.

docs.title: Ambisonics
docs.text:
• saf.decoder~ The saf.decoder~ object in Pure Data implements a frequency-dependent Ambisonic decoder for loudspeakers and headphones.
  • saf.encoder~ The saf.encoder~ object in Pure Data implements the Ambisonic encoder from the SPARTA VST3 plugins suite.

docs.title: saf.decoder~
docs.text:
The saf.decoder~ object in Pure Data implements a frequency-dependent Ambisonic decoder for loudspeakers and headphones. It supports up to 128 user-defined loudspeaker directions or presets for common 2D and 3D layouts. For headphones, it performs virtual loudspeaker decoding using interpolated HRTFs, with support for importing custom HRIRs via the SOFA standard. The decoder allows different settings for low and high frequencies with a user-defined crossover, offering integrated methods such as AllRAD, EPAD, and Mode-Matching, along with max-rE spatial weighting. It also provides options for adjusting decoding order and normalization (EP/AP) to maintain consistent loudness, which is useful for both practical and creative applications.

  • Download via Deken.

    Open Pd and go to ToolsFind Externals. Search for neimog and install it. Then create an object with declare -lib neimog -path neimog. Finally, use saf.decoder~ or any other object from neimog.

  • Library developed mainly by Charles K. Neimog.
  • Report Bugs/Errors here!
  • Available for .
Comments Contributors

docs.title: saf.encoder~
docs.text:
The saf.encoder~ object in Pure Data implements the Ambisonic encoder from the SPARTA VST3 plugins suite. It encodes up to 128 input channels into Ambisonic signals at specified directions, creating a synthetic sound scene whose spatial resolution depends on the encoding order. Presets are included for common multichannel formats (e.g., 22.x) to 1st–10th order Ambisonics. The object also provides a mouse-driven panning window with an equirectangular sphere representation for visualizing and adjusting azimuth and elevation of sources.

  • Download via Deken.

    Open Pd and go to ToolsFind Externals. Search for neimog and install it. Then create an object with declare -lib neimog -path neimog. Finally, use saf.encoder~ or any other object from neimog.

  • Library developed mainly by Charles K. Neimog.
  • Report Bugs/Errors here!
  • Available for .
Comments Contributors

docs.title: Audio I/O
docs.text:
• out~ out~ is a convenient Pure Data abstraction for managing audio output.
  • out8~ The out8~ object is an octaphonic (8-channel) audio output abstraction for Pure Data.
  • metronome~ The metronome~ object generates audio metronome clicks, working in conjunction with the metronome control object.
  • pix_pix2sig~ The pix_pix2sig~ object converts incoming image data, typically from GEM, into audio signals.
  • out4~ out4~ is a quadraphonic audio output abstraction for Pure Data, providing click-free control over DSP on/off, mute, and gain.
  • setdsp~ The setdsp~ object provides a convenient way to control Pure Data's audio engine (DSP) state.
  • bl.vsaw~ bl.vsaw~ is a bandlimited variable sawtooth waveform oscillator that can also generate triangular waveforms.
  • pvgrain~ pvgrain~ is a spectral granulator that tracks an input sound and synthesizes grains based on its spectral characteristics.
  • out.mc~ The out.mc~ object routes multichannel audio signals to specified dac~ output channels, with a default starting channel of 1.

docs.title: metronome~
docs.text:
The metronome~ object generates audio metronome clicks, working in conjunction with the metronome control object. It outputs these clicks as an audio signal, with configurable frequencies for low, mid, and high pitches. Different modes allow for variations in click patterns, such as suppressing subdivision clicks.

AI Generated

This content was generated with the assistance of AI. If you notice any errors, please report them or submit a fix using Submit. Check the prompt used here.

  • Download via Deken.

    Open Pd and go to ToolsFind Externals. Search for else and install it. Then create an object with declare -lib else -path else. Finally, use metronome~ or any other object from else.

  • Library developed mainly by Alexandre Torres Porres.

docs.title: out~
docs.text:
out~ is a convenient Pure Data abstraction for managing audio output. It provides controls for muting, toggling the DSP engine on/off, setting a ramp time for smooth gain changes, and adjusting maximum gain with various scaling modes (quartic, dB, linear). It also sums multichannel audio inputs to a stereo output.

AI Generated

docs.title: Binaural
docs.text:
• sofalizer~ SOFAlizer~ is a low-cost, interactive binaural system using real-time HRTF processing with Pure Data, supporting SOFA files and low-latency head tracking..
  • earplug~ earplug~ is a binaural filter based on KEMAR impulse measurement which allows you to spatialize a sound in realtime.

docs.title: earplug~
docs.text:
earplug~ is a binaural filter based on KEMAR impulse measurement which allows you to spatialize a sound in realtime. It basically takes the KEMAR dummy head data set, and interpolates 368 locations where HRTF measurement exists in a spherical surface. You get azimuth 0 - 360 degrees and elevation -40 - 90 degrees.

  • Download via Deken.
  • Library developed mainly by Pei Xiang, Jorge Castellanos, Hans-Christoph Steiner, Dan Wilcox, Chikashi Miyama.
  • Report Bugs/Errors here!
  • Available for .
Comments Contributors

docs.title: sofalizer~
docs.text:
SOFAlizer~ is a low-cost, interactive binaural system using real-time HRTF processing with Pure Data, supporting SOFA files and low-latency head tracking.

docs.title: Chorus/Flanger/Phaser
docs.text:
• chorus.m~ The chorus.m~ object is a wrapper around the chorus~ object from the ELSE library, designed to apply a chorus audio effect.
  • chorus~ chorus~ is a mono chorus effect that uses an internal comb filter to create a modulated delay.
  • flanger.m~ flanger.m~ is a Pure Data audio effect object that serves as a simplified wrapper for the flanger~ object from the ELSE library.
  • flanger~ The flanger~ object is a simple audio effect that creates a flanging sound.
  • phaser~ The phaser~ object is a mono phaser audio effect.
  • circuit~ The circuit~ object simulates analog electronic circuits from a text description.
  • phaser.m~ The phaser.m~ object is a wrapper for the phaser~ audio effect from the ELSE library.
  • delay~ The delay~ object delays an audio signal by a specified number of samples.

docs.title: chorus.m~
docs.text:
The chorus.m~ object is a wrapper around the chorus~ object from the ELSE library, designed to apply a chorus audio effect. It functions as a simple audio module, similar to a pedal, allowing control over parameters like rate, depth, and wetness.

AI Generated

docs.title: chorus~
docs.text:
chorus~ is a mono chorus effect that uses an internal comb filter to create a modulated delay. It allows control over the modulation rate, depth, and the dry/wet mix of the effect.

docs.title: flanger.m~
docs.text:
flanger.m~ is a Pure Data audio effect object that serves as a simplified wrapper for the flanger~ object from the ELSE library. It applies a flanging effect to an audio signal, allowing control over parameters such as modulation rate, depth, feedback coefficient, and wet/dry mix. This object functions as a straightforward audio processing module.

docs.title: flanger~
docs.text:
The flanger~ object is a simple audio effect that creates a flanging sound. It modulates a short delay line, with control over the modulation rate, depth, and a feedback/feedforward coefficient. The modulation waveform can be set to sine (default) or triangular.

docs.title: phaser.m~
docs.text:
The phaser.m~ object is a wrapper for the phaser~ audio effect from the ELSE library. It applies a phaser modulation effect to an incoming audio signal, with controls for rate, bandwidth, and wet/dry mix. This module functions like an audio pedal, currently without control voltage input.

docs.title: phaser~
docs.text:
The phaser~ object is a mono phaser audio effect. It allows control over the number of stages (which must be a multiple of 2), the Q-resonance, and the dry/wet mix. It requires a signal LFO connected to its second inlet to sweep the phaser's frequency.

docs.title: Control I/O
docs.text:
• keyboard The keyboard object provides a graphical piano keyboard interface for Pure Data.
  • gemmouse The gemmouse object captures and outputs mouse events occurring within a GEM (Graphics Environment for Multimedia) window.
  • touch.in The touch.in object extracts MIDI Aftertouch (channel pressure) information from either a connected MIDI device or an external raw MIDI data stream.
  • midi.in The midi.in object processes incoming MIDI data, converting raw MIDI streams into "cooked" data with type symbols, values, and channel information.
  • bend.in The bend.in object extracts MIDI Pitch Bend messages from connected MIDI devices or raw MIDI data streams.
  • gemtablet The gemtablet object in Pure Data interfaces with and responds to events from a graphics tablet.
  • keypress The keypress object detects presses of a single computer keyboard key, outputting a bang or a toggle (1 for pressed, 0 for released).
  • pix_video The pix_video object captures live video input from various cameras (USB, FireWire, capture cards) supported by the operating system.
  • mousestate The mousestate object reports the current state of the mouse, including click status, cursor X and Y positions, and delta movement.
  • pad The pad object is a graphical user interface (GUI) element that reports mouse coordinates and click status within its area.
  • canvas.mouse The canvas.mouse object captures mouse click status and coordinates within a Pure Data canvas.
  • gemorb The gemorb object connects to a SpaceOrb 3D controller via a specified serial port (comport).
  • numbox~ The numbox~ object is a graphical user interface element in Pure Data designed to display and generate audio signal values.
  • mouse The mouse object captures global mouse interaction within Pure Data.
  • gemw32window The gemw32window object is a GEM (Graphics Environment for Multimedia) object specific to Microsoft Windows, used for creating and managing a graphical output window.
  • mousefilter The mousefilter object acts as a gate, preventing messages from passing through while a mouse button is held down.
  • ctl.in The ctl.in object extracts MIDI Control Change (CC) messages from either a connected MIDI device or an external raw MIDI data stream.
  • gemkeyboard The gemkeyboard object captures keyboard events occurring within a GEM window.
  • gemsdl2window The gemsdl2window object creates and manages a graphical window using the SDL2 toolkit, providing an OpenGL context for rendering.
  • suspedal The suspedal object emulates a MIDI sustain pedal, holding Note Off messages when active and releasing them when deactivated.
  • knob The knob object is a graphical user interface (GUI) element in Pure Data that functions as a rotary control.
  • mpe.in The mpe.in object processes MIDI Polyphonic Expression (MPE) data, converting raw MIDI input from either an internal device or an external source into structured MPE messages.
  • midi.learn The midi.learn object is a Pure Data abstraction designed to learn and store incoming MIDI data, such as control changes, program changes, notes, and bends.
  • ptouch.in ptouch.in extracts MIDI polyphonic aftertouch messages from raw MIDI input, such as from a connected MIDI device or an external MIDI data stream.
  • click The click object sends a bang message when its parent patch (abstraction or subpatch) is clicked.
  • keymap The keymap object translates computer keyboard presses into MIDI note on/off messages, effectively turning your QWERTY keyboard into a musical input device.
  • gemkeyname The gemkeyname object captures keyboard events occurring within the GEM window.
  • keycode The keycode object provides layout-independent keyboard input, outputting a numerical key code based on the physical key location.
  • lpt The lpt object in Pure Data allows writing and reading data to and from a computer's parallel port.

docs.title: canvas.mouse
docs.text:
The canvas.mouse object captures mouse click status and coordinates within a Pure Data canvas. It can be configured to report coordinates relative to parent patches or subpatches, and to output data even when in edit mode.

docs.title: gemkeyboard
docs.text:
The gemkeyboard object captures keyboard events occurring within a GEM window. It outputs a KeyCode for each KEY_DOWN event, representing the pressed key. Users should be aware that KeyCode values may vary across different operating systems (Windows, Linux, OSX).

docs.text:

docs.title: gemmouse
docs.text:
The gemmouse object captures and outputs mouse events occurring within a GEM (Graphics Environment for Multimedia) window. It provides the X and Y pixel coordinates of the mouse cursor, with (0,0) at the top-left, and the state (pressed/released) of the left, middle, and right mouse buttons. Coordinates can optionally be normalized.

docs.text:
The keycode object provides layout-independent keyboard input, outputting a numerical key code based on the physical key location. It also indicates the key's status (1 for pressed, 0 for released) from its left outlet. This object is useful for mapping physical keyboard events in Pure Data patches.

docs.title: keypress
docs.text:
The keypress object detects presses of a single computer keyboard key, outputting a bang or a toggle (1 for pressed, 0 for released). It can be configured with a specific key as an argument or taught to "learn" a key press, filtering out autorepeated keys and those pressed in edit mode. This object is useful for triggering events or controlling parameters based on keyboard input.

docs.title: lpt
docs.text:
The lpt object in Pure Data allows writing and reading data to and from a computer's parallel port. It can write 8-bit words and read input bits, enabling direct hardware interfacing. Usage varies by operating system, often requiring specific drivers or root privileges, and it is not supported on macOS.

docs.text:
The mouse object captures global mouse interaction within Pure Data. It outputs the click status (1 for on, 0 for off) and the X/Y coordinates of the mouse. It can be reset or zeroed, and works for any click Pd receives in any window.

docs.title: mousefilter
docs.text:
The mousefilter object acts as a gate, preventing messages from passing through while a mouse button is held down. Upon release of the mouse button, it outputs the last message received during the click. This allows for filtering or capturing data based on mouse interaction.

docs.title: mousestate
docs.text:
The mousestate object reports the current state of the mouse, including click status, cursor X and Y positions, and delta movement. It can operate in different coordinate modes (screen, patch, or front-most patch relative) and provides outputs for these values. While click status is continuously reported, position and delta data require polling via a bang or poll message.

docs.title: Data Structures
docs.text:
• pack The pack object combines multiple incoming atoms (numbers, symbols, or pointers) from its inlets into a single list message.
  • symbol2list The symbol2list object converts an incoming symbol into a list.
  • presets The presets object in Pure Data allows users to save and recall multiple configurations of patch parameters, including GUI elements, numbers, symbols, and arrays.
  • insert The insert object allows you to insert a message or a list of elements into an existing list at a specified index.
  • sfload The sfload object loads sound files into Pure Data arrays, supporting various audio formats like MP3, WAV, and FLAC.
  • list2symbol The list2symbol object converts an incoming list into a single symbol.
  • glue The glue object combines two "packages" or messages, typically by appending or prepending one to the other.
  • store The store object is an abstraction built upon Pure Data's text object, designed for sequential storage and management of messages.
  • s2l The s2l object converts an input symbol into a list.
  • wave~ The wave~ object reads audio data from a specified array (wavetable) using a phase signal (0-1) as an index.
  • any2list The any2list object converts any incoming message into a list, standardizing various message types into a list format for further processing.
  • pack2 The pack2 object combines incoming data from its inlets into a single list, similar to pack.
  • lister The lister object (alias l) stores and outputs lists, and can also append one list to another.
  • index~ The index~ object reads values from an array without interpolation, using a signal as the index to read the array.
  • slice The slice object splits an incoming list into two parts based on a specified split point n.
  • urn The urn object is a unique random number generator.
  • buffer The buffer object provides a convenient way to get and set values within Pure Data arrays, including those created by array or table objects.
  • tabwriter~ The tabwriter~ object records incoming audio signals into Pure Data arrays.
  • lookup~ The lookup~ object performs a signal-rate table lookup, mapping input signal values (typically from -1 to 1) to indices within a specified array.
  • stack The stack object stores and manages sequences of messages, outputting them one by one upon receiving a bang.
  • zexy_index The index object creates and manages a symbol-to-integer map, allowing you to associate unique symbols with numerical indices.
  • unpack The unpack object splits an incoming message or list into its individual atoms, sending each atom to a separate outlet.
  • gemlist The gemlist object stores a list of GEM graphics commands or data.
  • msgfile The msgfile object reads and writes messages to text files, extending the functionality of textfile.
  • repack The repack object in Pure Data is used to (re)pack various data types—floats, symbols, or pointers—into fixed-size packages.
  • merge The merge object takes any type of messages from its inlets and combines them into a single list message.
  • unpack~ The unpack~ object converts a list of floating-point numbers into an audio signal.
  • pix_buffer_write The pix_buffer_write object writes incoming image data (a gemlist) into a specified pix_buffer at a given index.
  • thresh The thresh object collects incoming numbers and lists, combining them into a single output list if they arrive within a specified time interval (default 10ms).
  • tabdump The tabdump object outputs the entire contents of a Pure Data table as a list.
  • poke~ poke~ writes audio signals into a Pure Data array at specified indices.
  • sort The sort object sorts a list of floating-point numbers using a shell sort algorithm.
  • modelfiler The modelfiler object loads 3D model data from Alias/Wavefront OBJ files into Pure Data tables.
  • niagara The niagara object divides an incoming message or list into two sub-packages.
  • zl The zl object in Pure Data is a versatile list processor that manipulates messages containing one or more elements.
  • float2bits The float2bits object converts a decimal floating-point number (IEEE754 Single precision 32-bit) into its binary representation, outputting a list of 32 bits.
  • delace The delace object deinterleaves an incoming list into multiple sub-lists, distributing elements cyclically across its outlets.
  • pack~ The pack~ object converts incoming audio signals into lists of floating-point numbers.
  • tabset The tabset object from the zexy library allows users to set the contents of a Pure Data table.
  • iterate The iterate object splits an incoming message (typically a list of floats or symbols) into its individual elements and outputs them sequentially.
  • unmerge The unmerge object splits an incoming message into multiple sub-messages, distributing elements into groups of a specified size across its outlets.
  • join The join object in Pure Data combines messages from multiple inlets into a single output list.
  • buffer~ The buffer~ object stores audio data in memory as a multichannel array, supporting up to 64 channels.
  • multi.vsl The multi.vsl object is a graphical user interface (GUI) element that provides multiple vertical sliders in a single abstraction.
  • funbuff The funbuff object stores and manages ordered pairs of x/y integer values.
  • histo The histo object records the frequency of positive integer inputs, effectively building a histogram.
  • matchbox The matchbox object stores a collection of lists and retrieves them based on various matching algorithms.
  • group The group object collects incoming messages and outputs them as a single list once a specified group size is reached.
  • var The var object in Pure Data functions as a variable storage mechanism, similar to value, but with the added capability to manage multiple named variables, including lists.
  • l2i The l2i object converts all floating-point numbers within an incoming list to integers.
  • pak The pak object creates a list from its inlets, similar to pack.
  • l The l object (an alias for list) is used to store and manipulate lists in Pure Data.
  • length The length object, part of the zexy external library, determines the number of elements in an incoming list.
  • break The break object in Pure Data is used to split incoming messages, typically lists or symbols, into multiple parts based on a specified separator symbol.
  • coll The coll object in Pure Data is a versatile data collection and manipulation tool.
  • record~ The record~ object captures audio signals and stores them into Pure Data arrays, supporting up to 64 channels.
  • replace The replace object modifies an input message by substituting a specific element at a given index.
  • tabreader The tabreader object reads values from a Pure Data array (table), supporting various interpolation methods like lin, cos, and spline.
  • fifop The fifop object implements a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queue system that manages multiple internal queues, each capable of being assigned a floating-point priority.
  • unjoin The unjoin object separates an incoming list into smaller groups of elements.
  • offer The offer object stores temporary x/y number pairs.
  • capture The capture object stores incoming messages (floats and symbols) in the order they are received, acting as a sequential data buffer.
  • listfind The listfind object searches for occurrences of a sublist within a larger list.
  • lifop The lifop object manages multiple Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) stacks, each assigned a floating-point priority.
  • histogram The histogram object records the frequency of incoming positive integer numbers (floats are truncated) into an internal table.
  • lace The lace object interleaves elements from two or more input lists into a single output list.
  • peek~ The peek~ object provides direct, non-interpolated access to Pure Data arrays (tables) for both reading and writing values via messages.
  • tabgen tabgen is a Pure Data abstraction that generates various mathematical functions (e.g., sine, Gaussian, sinc, trapezoid, variable sawtooth, noise, and FFT window functions) and populates a specified table (array) with the generated data.
  • tabminmax The tabminmax object finds the minimum and maximum values within a Pure Data table.
  • a.rotate The a.rotate object in Pure Data appends a number to the end of an array, making it useful for tracking or visualizing the history of sound parameters over time..
  • uniqsym The uniqsym object removes duplicate symbols from a list or stream, maintaining a unique set of values.
  • remove The remove object filters elements from an input list.
  • packel The packel object extracts elements from a list, referred to as a "package." It can retrieve a specific element by its index (positive for from the beginning, negative for from the end), or the entire list if the index is 0.
  • pix_buffer pix_buffer is a named storage object for images, functioning similarly to a table but without direct visual access to its contents.
  • linear_path The linear_path object reads out values from a specified table.
  • l2s The list2symbol (or l2s) object converts an incoming list into a single symbol.
  • pmpd The pmpd object in Pure Data implements a mass-spring-damper physical model.
  • order The order object splits an incoming list into successive sub-lists of a specified size, assigning an ordered index to each.
  • pv The pv object in Pure Data functions as a private variable, storing any type of message.
  • table The table object in Pure Data stores and manipulates a numerical array.
  • list2lists The list2lists object splits an incoming list into multiple sublists.
  • batch.write~ The batch.write~ object is an abstraction built upon tabwriter~ that facilitates batch recording of audio signals into Pure Data arrays.
  • lastvalue The lastvalue object stores the most recently received input value.

It allows for flexible parsing of data, enabling different segments of a message to be processed independently. This is particularly useful for extracting arguments or sub-messages from complex data streams.

docs.text:
The buffer~ object stores audio data in memory as a multichannel array, supporting up to 64 channels. It enables reading from and writing to audio files (.wav, .aif, .caf), and its contents can be manipulated through messages for operations like waveform generation, gain adjustment, or applying windowing functions. It integrates with other Cyclone objects for playback and processing, and its internal arrays are accessible by vanilla Pd objects.

docs.title: capture
docs.text:
The capture object stores incoming messages (floats and symbols) in the order they are received, acting as a sequential data buffer. It allows users to view, edit, and save this stored data to a file. When the maximum capacity is reached, the oldest items are dropped, maintaining a first-in, first-out behavior. It also provides options for displaying numbers in various formats and controlling float precision.

docs.title: group
docs.text:
The group object collects incoming messages and outputs them as a single list once a specified group size is reached. If an input list exceeds the group size, the remaining elements form a remainder for subsequent grouping. A bang message forces the output of any accumulated remainder, and a group size of zero means it will only output upon receiving a bang.

docs.title: histo
docs.text:

docs.title: symbol2list
docs.text:
The symbol2list object converts an incoming symbol into a list. By default, it splits the symbol into list elements using spaces as delimiters. A different delimiter can be specified via its second inlet, and sending a bang to the object will re-parse the symbol with the current delimiter.

docs.title: tabdump
docs.text:
The tabdump object outputs the entire contents of a Pure Data table as a list. Triggered by a bang message, it provides a convenient way to access and process table data as a standard list, useful for inspection or further manipulation.

docs.title: table
docs.text:
The table object in Pure Data stores and manipulates a numerical array. It provides methods for graphical editing, setting and retrieving values, performing basic mathematical operations (e.g., sum, min, max), and loading/saving data from/to files. Multiple table instances can share the same data by assigning them a common name.

docs.title: tabminmax
docs.text:
The tabminmax object finds the minimum and maximum values within a Pure Data table. Additionally, it outputs the indices of these maximum values. It is part of the zexy library and is useful for analyzing data stored in tables.

docs.title: tabreader
docs.text:
The tabreader object reads values from a Pure Data array (table), supporting various interpolation methods like lin, cos, and spline. It can operate in a phase-based mode (0-1) or an indexed mode, and features multichannel and looping capabilities, making it suitable for wavetable synthesis or general array manipulation.

docs.text:

docs.title: tabwriter~
docs.text:
The tabwriter~ object records incoming audio signals into Pure Data arrays. It supports multi-channel recording (up to 64 channels) with sample accuracy, allowing control over recording start/end points, looping, and continuous recording. It's primarily used for capturing audio data into memory for further processing or analysis.

docs.title: thresh
docs.text:
The thresh object collects incoming numbers and lists, combining them into a single output list if they arrive within a specified time interval (default 10ms). It appends each new item to the previously stored ones, resetting its internal timer with every incoming message. This is useful for grouping related data that arrives in close temporal proximity.

docs.title: uniqsym
docs.text:
The uniqsym object removes duplicate symbols from a list or stream, maintaining a unique set of values. It can also sort the collected symbols and output them either as a single list or as a stream of individual symbols. This object is useful for managing unique textual data within Pure Data patches.

docs.title: unjoin
docs.text:
The unjoin object separates an incoming list into smaller groups of elements. Each group is sent to a dedicated outlet, with any remaining elements routed to an additional "extra" outlet. The size of these groups can be configured using the @outsize attribute or an argument.

docs.text:

docs.title: unpack
docs.text:
The unpack object splits an incoming message or list into its individual atoms, sending each atom to a separate outlet. Similar to Pure Data's built-in unpack object, the number and type of expected atoms are defined by its creation arguments. This object is useful for deconstructing structured data into its constituent parts for further processing.

docs.title: unpack~
docs.text:
The unpack~ object converts a list of floating-point numbers into an audio signal. It takes a list of floats as its inlet and outputs a signal. An optional creation argument bufsize (defaulting to 64 samples) can be used to control the buffer size, effectively acting as a simple sample rate reducer.

docs.title: var
docs.text:
The var object in Pure Data functions as a variable storage mechanism, similar to value, but with the added capability to manage multiple named variables, including lists. It allows setting and recalling values for these variables, which are shared with value objects and can be accessed within expr objects. Values are typically assigned to var objects via send messages or other sending mechanisms from objects like float, int, or value.

docs.title: wave~
docs.text:
The wave~ object reads audio data from a specified array (wavetable) using a phase signal (0-1) as an index. It offers seven different interpolation modes, including linear, cosine, cubic, and Hermite, allowing for precise control over playback quality. Additionally, wave~ supports setting custom start and end points within the array and can handle multichannel audio up to 64 channels.

docs.text:

It supports adding new items, retrieving an item's index, and optionally auto-adding unknown symbols. This object is useful for efficiently mapping textual identifiers to numerical representations within a Pure Data patch.

docs.title: zl
docs.text:
The zl object in Pure Data is a versatile list processor that manipulates messages containing one or more elements. It offers numerous modes for common list operations such as slicing, grouping, sorting, filtering, joining, and searching. Users can configure its behavior via arguments, messages, or by appending the mode name with a dot (e.g., zl.slice).

docs.title: Deep Learning
docs.text: • nn~ nn~ is a bridge between Max/MSP or PureData and LibTorch (PyTorch C++ interface) for deep learning.

docs.title: nn~
docs.text:
nn~ is a bridge between Max/MSP or PureData and LibTorch (PyTorch C++ interface) for deep learning. It does not work alone and requires pretrained models.

Key Points
  • Purpose: Allows deep learning models to run inside Max/MSP or PureData.
  • Dependencies: Pretrained models in TorchScript (.ts) format.
  • Model Management:
  • Place .ts files in nn_tilde/models or any folder accessible through Max/Pd filesystem:
    • Max: Options → File Preferences
    • PureData: File → Preferences → Path
  • Since v1.6.0, some models can be downloaded via IRCAM Forum API.
  • Usage: Load a model by specifying its name as the first argument to nn~:
  • Download here or use Deken..
  • Library developed mainly by ACIDS/Ircam.
  • Report Bugs/Errors here!
  • Available for .
Articles

  • Hierarchical temporal learning for multi-instrument and orchestral audio synthesis by Hierarchical temporal learning for multi-instrument and orchestral audio synthesis

    Link

Videos Comments Contributors

docs.title: Delay
docs.text:
• pix_delay The pix_delay object creates a frame-based delay line for images, allowing a series of images to be delayed.
  • fbdelay~ The fbdelay~ object creates a feedback delay line, useful for delay effects, reverberation, and comb filtering.
  • pipe2 The pipe2 object functions similarly to Pure Data's vanilla pipe object, delaying incoming messages.
  • echo.rev~ echo.rev~ is an echo/reverb abstraction designed to produce early reflections in a reverb algorithm or function as a standalone effect.
  • trig.delay2~ The trig.delay2~ object delays an incoming impulse signal by a specified time in milliseconds.
  • del~ The del~ object in Pure Data manages audio delay lines.
  • ping.pong~ The ping.pong~ object is an audio effect that creates a ping-pong delay.
  • z~ The z~ object provides a sample-wise delay for audio signals, effectively implementing a z^-N delay.
  • teeth~ The teeth~ object implements a comb filter, processing an audio signal with independent feedforward and feedback delays.
  • filterdelay~ The filterdelay~ object is a high-level audio delay unit that incorporates a resonant lowpass filter, a soft clipper, and a DC filter within its feedback path.
  • delay.m~ delay.m~ is an audio effect object that functions as a wrapper for the filterdelay~ object from the ELSE library.
  • gatehold~ The gatehold~ object holds an incoming gate signal for a specified duration in milliseconds after the gate closes.
  • revdelay~ The revdelay~ object creates a reverse delay effect.
  • ffdelay~ The ffdelay~ object provides a simple feed-forward audio delay with interpolation.
  • comb~ The comb~ object implements a comb filter, an audio effect that combines a signal with a delayed version of itself to create a series of regularly spaced notches or peaks in the frequency response.
  • comb.rev~ The comb.rev~ object implements a comb filter, functioning as a versatile audio effect for creating delays, reverberation, and resonant textures.
  • gatedelay The gatedelay object delays the "on" message of a control gate by a specified time in milliseconds, while passing the "off" message immediately.
  • allpass~ The allpass~ object implements an all-pass filter, which passes all frequencies without altering their gain but changes their phase.
  • allpass.rev~ The allpass.rev~ object functions as an allpass filter, a delay, and a reverberator.
  • comb.filt~ The comb.filt~ object implements a feedback comb filter, designed primarily for use as a resonator.
  • delay~ The delay~ object delays an audio signal by a specified number of samples.

docs.title: allpass~
docs.text:
The allpass~ object implements an all-pass filter, which passes all frequencies without altering their gain but changes their phase. It is primarily used for filtering and delay effects, with configurable delay time and a gain coefficient. The object supports dynamic control of delay and gain via inlets and can be initialized with arguments for maximum delay, initial delay, and gain.

docs.title: comb.filt~
docs.text:
The comb.filt~ object implements a feedback comb filter, designed primarily for use as a resonator. It processes an audio signal by adding a delayed and attenuated version of itself, creating a series of notches and peaks in the frequency response. Parameters include delay time (in Hz or milliseconds) and a decay coefficient, which can be set as a 60dB decay time (t60) or a direct gain value.

docs.title: comb~
docs.text:
The comb~ object implements a comb filter, an audio effect that combines a signal with a delayed version of itself to create a series of regularly spaced notches or peaks in the frequency response. It is primarily used for filtering and creating delay-based effects, with adjustable parameters for delay time (D), input gain (a), feedforward gain (b), and feedback gain (c).

docs.title: delay~
docs.text:
The delay~ object delays an audio signal by a specified number of samples. The delay time can be set by a float (for non-interpolated delays) or a signal (for interpolated, dynamic delays, which adds a 1-sample latency). It also supports messages to clear its buffer, set a maximum delay size, and smoothly ramp to new delay times, making it fundamental for creating effects like vibrato and flanger.

docs.title: del~
docs.text:
The del~ object in Pure Data manages audio delay lines. When instantiated as del~ in, it defines and writes a signal to a delay line, allowing control over its size and freezing. When instantiated as del~ out, it reads from a defined delay line with cubic interpolation, enabling flexible audio delays.

docs.title: filterdelay~
docs.text:
The filterdelay~ object is a high-level audio delay unit that incorporates a resonant lowpass filter, a soft clipper, and a DC filter within its feedback path. It allows for control over delay time, feedback amount, filter cutoff, resonance, and wet/dry mix. The object can also be frozen or cleared, making it suitable for creating complex, filtered echo and reverb-like effects.

docs.text:

It ignores any subsequent triggers received before the delayed output is sent, effectively acting as a retriggerable delay. This object supports multichannel signals for both the trigger input and delay time.

docs.title: z~
docs.text:
The z~ object provides a sample-wise delay for audio signals, effectively implementing a z^-N delay. It is primarily designed to facilitate the creation of Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filters, such as a moving average filter, by delaying the input signal by a specified number of samples.

AI Generated

This content was generated with the assistance of AI. If you notice any errors, please report them or submit a fix using Submit. Check the prompt used here.

    Open Pd and go to ToolsFind Externals. Search for zexy and install it. Then create an object with declare -lib zexy -path zexy. Finally, use z~ or any other object from zexy.

docs.title: Descriptors
docs.text:
• peakSample The peakSample object analyzes a specified audio array or a segment of it to find the maximum sample value and its corresponding index.

  • spectrograph~ The spectrograph~ object visualizes the FFT amplitudes of an incoming audio signal, displaying frequencies from 0 Hz to Nyquist.

  • pix_multiblob The pix_multiblob object detects multiple "blobs" within an image, defined as adjacent pixels exceeding a specified luminance threshold and minimum blobSize.
  • range~ The range~ object analyzes an incoming audio signal to continuously output its minimum and maximum amplitude values.

  • pix_blobtracker The pix_blobtracker object in Pure Data is a video analysis tool designed to detect and track multiple distinct regions (blobs) within an incoming video stream.
  • attackTime~ The attackTime~ object calculates the attack time of an audio event in milliseconds.
  • chroma~ The chroma~ object analyzes incoming audio to determine the spectral energy present in each of the 12 musical pitch classes, regardless of octave.
  • specSlope The specSlope object calculates the spectral slope of an audio sample, quantifying the average decrease in amplitude across increasing frequencies.
  • cepstrumPitch~ The cepstrumPitch~ object performs real-time pitch tracking of an audio signal using cepstrum analysis.
  • dfreq~ The dfreq~ object is a computationally efficient frequency detector that estimates the frequency of an incoming audio signal by counting zero-crossings.
  • attackTime The attackTime object analyzes a specified segment of a sound sample array to determine its attack time.
  • minSampleDelta The minSampleDelta object analyzes a specified window within a sample array to find the smallest absolute difference between consecutive samples.
  • tID_fft The tID_fft object performs an offline Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) on a specified segment of an audio sample array in Pure Data.
  • maxSampleDelta~ The maxSampleDelta~ object analyzes an audio stream to find the largest absolute difference between adjacent samples within a specified window.
  • barkSpecCentroid The barkSpecCentroid object computes the Bark spectrum centroid, a psychoacoustically weighted measure of an audio signal's spectral balance.
  • waveSlope~ The waveSlope~ object calculates the slope of the best-fit line through the absolute value of audio samples within a configurable analysis window.
  • specSpread The specSpread object calculates the spectral spread, a measure of the dispersion of the spectrum around its centroid, for an audio signal stored in a Pure Data array.
  • specCentroid The specCentroid object calculates the spectral centroid of an audio array, providing a measure of the 'brightness' or 'timbral richness' of a sound.
  • zeroCrossing The zeroCrossing object calculates the zero-crossing rate of a specified audio array or a segment of it.
  • autoCorrPitch The autoCorrPitch object performs offline pitch tracking on a specified audio sample array using an autocorrelation algorithm.
  • nyquist~ The nyquist~ object reports the Nyquist frequency (half the audio sample rate) or its corresponding period.
  • mov.rms~ The mov.rms~ object calculates the Root Mean Square (RMS) of an incoming audio signal over a specified moving time window.

  • pvoc~ The pvoc~ object performs phase vocoding, a technique for time-stretching or pitch-shifting audio by analyzing its frequency content.
  • waveNoise~ The waveNoise~ object measures the noisiness of an audio signal by counting the number of times the signal changes direction within a specified window.
  • specBrightness~ The specBrightness~ object calculates the spectral brightness of an audio signal, defined as the ratio of high-frequency spectral magnitudes to total spectral magnitudes.

  • barkSpec~ The barkSpec~ object analyzes incoming audio to compute its Bark-frequency spectrum, which warps the normal spectrum to the Bark scale, emphasizing low-frequency detail.

docs.title: attackTime
docs.text:
The attackTime object analyzes a specified segment of a sound sample array to determine its attack time. It identifies the onset and peak of a sound event within the given window, outputting the attack time in milliseconds, along with the sample indices for the onset start and peak. Users can configure parameters such as sample rate, search range, and magnitude thresholds for precise onset detection.

docs.title: attackTime~
docs.text:
The attackTime~ object calculates the attack time of an audio event in milliseconds. Upon receiving a bang, it identifies the peak sample within its analysis window and then searches backward for the attack onset, defined by a period of near-silence. This object requires an external onset detector to trigger its analysis and outputs the attack time, onset, and peak sample indices.

docs.title: autoCorrPitch
docs.text:
The autoCorrPitch object performs offline pitch tracking on a specified audio sample array using an autocorrelation algorithm. It analyzes a segment of the array (or the entire array) and outputs the detected fundamental frequency as a MIDI pitch value. It can be configured with a sample rate and a correlation threshold for detection.

docs.title: autoCorrPitch~
docs.text:
The autoCorrPitch~ object calculates the fundamental pitch of an incoming audio signal using autocorrelation, converting the result to Hertz and MIDI units. It is designed for real-time pitch tracking and can be used as a feature extractor for timbre identification. Users can adjust the analysis window size to optimize accuracy and the range of detectable frequencies.

docs.title: bark
docs.text:
The bark object is a non-real-time audio onset detector that identifies musical attacks by analyzing spectral changes. It employs a spectral differencing growth algorithm, weighted by the perceptually-based Bark frequency scale, which can improve accuracy for signals with significant resonance. It outputs onset times, total energy growth, and growth per Bark band, facilitating detailed audio analysis and segmentation.

docs.title: barkSpec
docs.text:
The barkSpec object calculates the Bark spectrum of an audio sample or a specified segment within a sample array. It allows configuration of analysis parameters such as window function and filterbank spacing. This object is useful for psychoacoustically informed audio analysis, particularly for timbre description.

docs.title: barkSpecBrightness
docs.text:
The barkSpecBrightness object calculates the brightness of an audio sample by analyzing its Bark spectrum. It takes an audio array, filterbank spacing, and a Bark boundary point as arguments to customize the spectral analysis. This object is designed for efficient descriptor extraction within signal processing chains, capable of accepting pre-computed FFT, magnitude, or Bark spectrum data.

docs.title: barkSpecBrightness~
docs.text:
The barkSpecBrightness~ object calculates the brightness of an audio signal's Bark spectrum. It determines brightness as the ratio of energy above a specified Bark frequency boundary to the total energy in the spectrum. This descriptor is useful for real-time timbre identification and can be configured with various windowing functions, filterbank spacings, and boundary points.

docs.title: barkSpecCentroid~
docs.text:
The barkSpecCentroid~ object calculates the Bark spectrum centroid, representing the "center of mass" of an audio signal's energy distribution on the Bark frequency scale. It outputs a numerical value indicating this Bark frequency, making it suitable for real-time timbre identification and spectral analysis. The object handles its own audio buffering and windowing, with configurable window size and Bark-spacing.

docs.title: barkSpecFlatness
docs.text:
The barkSpecFlatness object calculates the spectral flatness of an audio signal within Bark-scaled frequency bands. It analyzes a specified window of a sample array, providing a measure of how noise-like or tone-like the sound is in perceptually relevant frequency regions. It supports various analysis parameters like windowing functions and can process pre-computed spectral data.

docs.title: barkSpecFlux
docs.text:
The barkSpecFlux object calculates the spectral flux between two audio windows (forward and back) based on their Bark band energy differences. It sums these per-Bark-band energy differences, providing a measure of how quickly the spectral content of an audio signal is changing. This object is highly configurable, allowing adjustments to windowing, filterbank spacing, and the type of difference calculation, and can operate in flux, growth, or decay modes.

docs.title: barkSpecFlux~
docs.text:
The barkSpecFlux~ object calculates the spectral flux of an audio signal based on Bark-band energy differences. It outputs the sum of these differences (left outlet) and the full list of per-band differences (right outlet). This object is useful for real-time timbre identification and basic attack detection, offering configurable windowing, Bark filterbank spacing, and analysis window separation.

docs.title: barkSpecIrregularity
docs.text:
The barkSpecIrregularity object computes the Bark spectrum irregularity of an audio signal, a descriptor indicating the noisiness or irregularity of its spectral envelope. It processes a specified window of an audio array, offering configurable parameters such as Bark filterbank spacing, windowing function, and a choice between Jensen or Krimphoff algorithms. For efficiency, it can also accept pre-computed spectral data via "chain" messages.

docs.text:
The barkSpecIrregularity~ object calculates the spectral irregularity of an audio signal's Bark spectrum. It quantifies how 'jagged' or 'smooth' the energy distribution is across Bark bands, using either the Jensen or Krimphoff algorithm. This descriptor is primarily used for real-time timbre analysis and classification.

docs.title: barkSpecKurtosis
docs.text:

It analyzes audio signals or specified portions of sample arrays, providing a statistical measure of the "peakedness" of the Bark spectrum. The object supports various windowing functions and can efficiently process pre-computed spectral data.

docs.title: barkSpecRolloff
docs.text:

It analyzes a specified window of an audio array, using a given Bark filterbank spacing and a concentration threshold, and outputs a numerical value representing this descriptor. It can also process pre-computed Bark spectrum data.

docs.title: barkSpecRolloff~
docs.text:
The barkSpecRolloff~ object calculates the Bark spectrum rolloff, which is the Bark frequency below which a specified concentration of spectral energy is located. It processes audio in real-time, handling its own buffering and windowing, and allows configuration of window size, filterbank spacing, and the energy concentration threshold. This descriptor is useful for real-time timbre analysis.

A positive value indicates more energy in lower frequencies, while a negative value signifies more energy in higher frequencies. docs.text:
The barkSpecSpread~ object calculates the spread of an audio signal's Bark spectrum, indicating the concentration of energy around its centroid. It outputs this value in Barks, where a low spread signifies concentrated energy (like a sinusoid) and a high spread indicates dispersed energy (like noise). This descriptor is useful for analyzing timbre and can serve as a feature for real-time audio classification systems.

docs.title: bark~
docs.text:
The bark~ object is a real-time onset detector for Pure Data, which analyzes spectral growth using the perceptually-based Bark frequency scale. It outputs a bang upon detecting an onset, along with the total energy growth and a list of growth values per Bark band. This object is ideal for applications requiring robust transient detection in audio signals.

This object is useful for extracting perceptually relevant features from audio for tasks like timbre identification.

docs.title: binWrangler
docs.text:
The binWrangler Pure Data object accumulates a specified number of feature vector frames, such as BFCCs, and then outputs the time-varying information organized by bin number as a concatenated list. It can also operate in a "spew" mode, acting as a sliding buffer that outputs the accumulated data with each new frame. This object is primarily used for processing sequential feature data in the timbreID library.

This object is designed for non-real-time audio feature extraction and can integrate into analysis chains by accepting pre-computed FFT or magnitude spectrum data.

docs.title: dfreq~
docs.text:
The dfreq~ object is a computationally efficient frequency detector that estimates the frequency of an incoming audio signal by counting zero-crossings. It updates its frequency estimation at every zero-crossing. While it is much cheaper than FFT-based methods, it is not designed to work properly with complex signals.

docs.title: nearestPoint
docs.text:
The nearestPoint object identifies the closest point(s) within a stored dataset to a given input point in a multi-dimensional space. It builds its dataset by receiving points in its left inlet and, upon receiving a query point in its right inlet, outputs the index and distance of the nearest match(es). The dimensionality of points is defined at creation, and the number of returned matches can be specified.

docs.title: specFlux~
docs.text:
The specFlux~ object calculates the spectral flux of an audio signal, quantifying how quickly its spectrum changes over time. It offers options for measuring total flux, spectral growth, or decay, and outputs both a summed value and detailed bin-by-bin differences. This object is useful for analyzing transient events and can serve as an input for timbre identification.

docs.title: specHarmonicity
docs.text:
The specHarmonicity object calculates the spectral harmonicity and inharmonicity of an audio sample. It analyzes the spectral content, optionally using a provided fundamental frequency, to quantify how closely the partials align with a harmonic series. This object is primarily used for extracting timbral descriptors from audio.

docs.title: specHarmonicity~
docs.text:
The specHarmonicity~ object quantifies the harmonic alignment of spectral peaks in an audio signal, outputting both harmonicity and inharmonicity values between 0 and 1. It identifies significant spectral peaks and assesses how well they align with a given fundamental frequency, which can be supplied externally (e.g., from sigmund~) or automatically derived from the lowest non-DC spectral peak. This object is useful for extracting timbral features for real-time sound analysis and classification.

docs.title: specIrregularity
docs.text:
The specIrregularity object calculates the spectral irregularity of an audio signal, providing a numerical descriptor of its timbral characteristics. It supports both Jensen and Krimphoff algorithms, analyzing full audio arrays or specific windows with various windowing functions. Designed for efficiency, it can integrate into analysis chains by accepting pre-computed FFT or magnitude spectrum data.

docs.title: specKurtosis
docs.text:
The specKurtosis object calculates the spectral kurtosis of an audio array, serving as a non-real-time spectral descriptor. It can analyze a specified window or the entire array, with configurable windowing functions, sample rates, and spectrum types (magnitude or power). A key feature is its ability to accept pre-computed FFT or magnitude spectrum data via chain_ messages for efficient chained analysis workflows.

docs.title: specKurtosis~
docs.text:
The specKurtosis~ object calculates the spectral kurtosis of an audio signal, quantifying the peakedness of its spectrum. It is useful for distinguishing between tonal and noisy sounds, with higher values indicating more peaked spectra (like a sine wave) and lower values for flatter spectra (like noise). This descriptor can be used for real-time timbre identification and offers configurable windowing and spectrum type (magnitude or power).

docs.text:

A positive value indicates more energy in lower frequencies, while a negative value suggests more energy in higher frequencies. It handles its own audio buffering and windowing, making it suitable for real-time spectral analysis and feature extraction for timbre classification.

docs.title: spectrograph~
docs.text:
The spectrograph~ object visualizes the FFT amplitudes of an incoming audio signal, displaying frequencies from 0 Hz to Nyquist. It uses a Hann window for spectral analysis and allows customization of FFT size, amplitude scaling (linear or dB), frequency scaling (linear or logarithmic), and display dimensions. When its graph rate is set to 0, it can be triggered manually by a bang message.

docs.text:

It allows conversion between linear frequencies (Hz), psychoacoustic Bark and Mel scales, and Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) bin numbers. These conversions are fundamental for working with spectral audio features and descriptors.

docs.title: tID_fft
docs.text:
The tID_fft object performs an offline Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) on a specified segment of an audio sample array in Pure Data. It offers configurable windowing functions, normalization, and zero-padding for detailed spectral analysis. The object outputs the real and imaginary components of the FFT, which are useful for further frequency-domain processing or visualization.

docs.title: timbreID
docs.text:
The timbreID object stores, clusters, and classifies audio feature vectors, enabling tasks like sound identification and timbre ordering. It performs nearest-neighbor searches and hierarchical clustering on incoming feature data. This object is particularly useful for real-time concatenative synthesis and analyzing large datasets of pre-recorded audio.

docs.text:

docs.title: zeroCrossing~
docs.text:
The zeroCrossing~ object measures the noisiness or frequency-related characteristics of an audio signal by counting how many times it crosses the zero amplitude point within a specified window. It outputs this count as a numerical feature, often used as a crude timbre descriptor. Users can set the analysis window size via a creation argument or a 'window' message, and it's recommended to apply an amplitude threshold before measurement to avoid low-level noise interference.

docs.title: Distortion
docs.text:
• drive.m~ drive.m~ is a multichannel audio object that applies a distortion or overdrive effect to incoming audio signals.
  • crusher.m~ The crusher.m~ object is a multichannel audio effect that wraps the crusher~ object from the ELSE library.
  • digidistort The digidistort object provides digital distortion effects for audio signals.
  • lookup~ The lookup~ object performs a signal-rate table lookup, mapping input signal values (typically from -1 to 1) to indices within a specified array.
  • rubber The rubber object is a GEM object that renders and distorts a square.
  • tanh~ The tanh~ object calculates the hyperbolic tangent of an input audio signal.
  • fold~ The fold~ object performs wavefolding, a waveshaping technique that mirrors audio signals back into a specified low and high range.
  • shaper~ The shaper~ object performs waveshaping by applying a user-defined transfer function to an input signal.
  • drive~ The drive~ object in Pure Data simulates analog soft-clipping distortion by applying various non-linear transfer functions.
  • circuit~ The circuit~ object simulates analog electronic circuits from a text description.
  • crusher~ The crusher~ object is an audio effect that performs bit-crushing and decimation on an input signal.
  • overdrive~ The overdrive~ object simulates analog overdrive by applying a non-linear transfer function to an incoming audio signal, mimicking the soft clipping of a tube-based circuit.
  • kink~ The kink~ object distorts a phasor~ signal by applying a "kink" to its phase.
  • rm~ The rm~ object performs ring modulation on an input signal.

docs.title: circuit~
docs.text:
The circuit~ object simulates analog electronic circuits from a text description. It allows audio signals to modulate circuit parameters via inlets and outputs audio from designated probe points within the simulated circuit. This object can be CPU intensive due to the complexity of the simulations.

docs.title: crusher~
docs.text:
The crusher~ object is an audio effect that performs bit-crushing and decimation on an input signal. It introduces distortion and aliasing by quantizing and downsampling the audio. Its two parameters, bit reduction and decimation, range from 0 (original sound) to 1 (maximum effect).

docs.title: digidistort
docs.text:
The digidistort object provides digital distortion effects for audio signals. It includes quantize~ for quantizing a signal with a variable step number and swap~ for byte-swapping a signal after converting it to 16-bit. These functionalities are designed to create distinct "digital" sounding distortions.

docs.title: drive~
docs.text:
The drive~ object in Pure Data simulates analog soft-clipping distortion by applying various non-linear transfer functions. It processes an audio signal input, using a 'drive factor' to control the intensity and character of the distortion. Different modes offer distinct distortion curves, effectively shaping the signal's dynamics and adding harmonic content.

docs.title: kink~
docs.text:
The kink~ object distorts a phasor~ signal by applying a "kink" to its phase. It modifies the signal's slope based on a threshold, creating a non-linear transformation. This allows for unique timbral changes by altering the fundamental waveform of an oscillator.

docs.title: lookup~
docs.text:
The lookup~ object performs a signal-rate table lookup, mapping input signal values (typically from -1 to 1) to indices within a specified array. It is primarily used for waveshaping, where an array acts as a transfer function to distort an input signal. Additionally, it can be employed for creative sample manipulation, such as scanning through audio samples to create effects like turntable scratches.

docs.title: overdrive~
docs.text:
The overdrive~ object simulates analog overdrive by applying a non-linear transfer function to an incoming audio signal, mimicking the soft clipping of a tube-based circuit. It takes an audio signal and a "drive factor" as input, producing a distorted output signal. Increasing the drive factor intensifies the distortion.

docs.title: rubber
docs.text:
The rubber object is a GEM object that renders and distorts a square. When a vertex is 'grabbed' by a bang, it can be moved, causing adjacent vertices to follow inertly, creating a rubber-like distortion effect. Another bang releases the vertex, returning it to its original position.

docs.title: Dynamics
docs.text:
• expand~ The expand~ object functions as an audio expander, attenuating an input signal when its level falls below a specified threshold.
  • pvcompand~ pvcompand~ is a spectral compander that either expands or compresses the dynamic range of audio signals in the frequency domain.
  • duck~ duck~ is a Pure Data object that implements sidechain compression, attenuating an input audio signal based on the level of a separate control signal.
  • autofade2~ autofade2~ is a Pure Data object that provides automatic fade-in and fade-out functionality for multiple audio inputs.
  • dbgain~ The dbgain~ object adjusts the gain of an audio signal to a specified decibel (dB) level.
  • drown~ The drown~ object performs spectral noise floor manipulation on audio signals using FFT.
  • norm~ norm~ is an audio normalizer that adjusts the loudness of an incoming signal to a specified decibel full scale (dBFS) level.
  • level~ The level~ object is a convenient tool for adjusting the gain of audio signals in decibels (dB).
  • compress~ The compress~ object is an audio compressor abstraction that attenuates an input signal when its level exceeds a specified threshold.
  • limiter~ The limiter~ object in Pure Data functions as an audio dynamics processor, preventing signals from exceeding a specified output level to avoid clipping.
  • noisegate~ noisegate~ is a noise gate abstraction that allows audio signals to pass only when their RMS value exceeds a specified threshold in dBFS.
  • autofade~ The autofade~ object provides automatic fade-in and fade-out functionality for multiple audio inputs.
  • peakamp~ The peakamp~ object measures the absolute peak amplitude of an incoming audio signal.

docs.title: compress~
docs.text:
The compress~ object is an audio compressor abstraction that attenuates an input signal when its level exceeds a specified threshold. It allows control over compression ratio, attack and release times, and output gain, also providing an outlet for the amount of gain reduction.

docs.title: dbgain~
docs.text:
The dbgain~ object adjusts the gain of an audio signal to a specified decibel (dB) level. It functions similarly to level~ but without a graphical user interface, making it suitable for programmatic control. A key feature is its multichannel support, allowing different dB levels to be applied to individual channels via its right inlet.

docs.title: level~
docs.text:

docs.title: limiter~
docs.text:
The limiter~ object in Pure Data functions as an audio dynamics processor, preventing signals from exceeding a specified output level to avoid clipping. It can operate as a single-threshold limiter, a two-threshold "crack" limiter, or a compressor, with all levels defined in dB. Due to its algorithm, it requires a small internal delay on the input signal for proper operation.

docs.title: Envelopes
docs.text:
• grain.sampler~ The grain.sampler~ object is a sample-based granulator that generates clouds of audio grains from a loaded sample.
  • grain.live~ grain.live~ is a live input granulator that generates clouds of audio grains from an incoming signal.
  • envgen~ envgen~ is a versatile audio envelope and line generator that creates complex amplitude curves based on lists of duration and target values.
  • grain.synth~ grain.synth~ is a waveform-based granular synthesizer that generates clouds of pitched grains.
  • autofade.mc~ The autofade.mc~ object provides automatic fade-in and fade-out functionality for multichannel audio signals.
  • autofade2.mc~ The autofade2.mc~ object provides automatic fade-in and fade-out functionality for multichannel audio signals.
  • autofade2~ autofade2~ is a Pure Data object that provides automatic fade-in and fade-out functionality for multiple audio inputs.
  • asr~ The asr~ object generates an attack/sustain/release envelope, acting as a simplified version of adsr~.
  • curve~ curve~ is a signal-rate object that generates non-linear (curved) ramp signals, functioning as an envelope generator.
  • adsr~ adsr~ is an audio-rate Attack, Decay, Sustain, Release envelope generator.
  • function The function object is a graphical user interface (GUI) for creating and manipulating breakpoint functions.
  • envelope~ The envelope~ object generates various single-cycle envelope waveforms (e.g., sine, Hanning, triangular, Gaussian, trapezoidal) based on a phase input.
  • ramp~ The ramp~ object generates a linear signal that ramps between a specified minimum and maximum value.
  • peak~ peak~ is a Pure Data signal object that analyzes an incoming audio signal and outputs its peak amplitude.
  • decay~ decay~ is a one-pole filter that generates an exponential decay from input impulses.
  • envrms~ The envrms~ object is an envelope follower designed for audio signals.
  • autofade~ The autofade~ object provides automatic fade-in and fade-out functionality for multiple audio inputs.
  • adsr.m~ The adsr.m~ object is a polyphonic ADSR envelope generator.
  • line~ The cyclone/line~ object generates linear signal ramps or envelopes.

docs.title: adsr.m~
docs.text:
The adsr.m~ object is a polyphonic ADSR envelope generator. It accepts multichannel gate signals in its left inlet and can be retriggered by impulses or bangs in its second inlet. Attack, decay, and release times are specified in milliseconds, while the sustain level is a ratio of the gate input, with additional audio CV controls for each stage.

docs.text:

It shapes incoming gate signals into a dynamic amplitude curve, with configurable attack, decay, sustain level, and release times. The object supports retriggering, immediate release, multichannel processing, and adjustable curve shapes for its segments.

docs.title: asr~
docs.text:
The asr~ object generates an attack/sustain/release envelope, acting as a simplified version of adsr~. It shapes an audio signal based on gate input (signal or bang), with adjustable attack and release times in milliseconds. The object supports various envelope curve shapes (linear, logarithmic, or lag filter) and provides a status outlet indicating its active state, making it useful for dynamic control and multichannel applications.

docs.title: envrms~
docs.text:
The envrms~ object is an envelope follower designed for audio signals. Unlike the standard env~ object which outputs decibel values, envrms~ directly calculates and outputs the Root Mean Square (RMS) amplitude of the incoming signal as a float. This simplifies patch design by providing a direct measurement of signal power, often eliminating the need for a separate dbtorms conversion.

docs.text:
The cyclone/line~ object generates linear signal ramps or envelopes. It smoothly transitions its signal output from the current value to a target value over a specified duration in milliseconds. It can process single target-duration pairs or lists of multiple pairs to create complex envelopes, and it sends a bang upon reaching the final target.

docs.title: peak~
docs.text:
peak~ is a Pure Data signal object that analyzes an incoming audio signal and outputs its peak amplitude. Similar to env~, it can be configured with an analysis window size and hop period, and its output can be set to linear or decibels (dBFS).

docs.title: ramp~
docs.text:
The ramp~ object generates a linear signal that ramps between a specified minimum and maximum value. It can be configured with an increment, a reset point, and various modes (wrap, clip, reset) to control its behavior upon reaching boundaries. This versatile object is useful for creating envelopes, controlling playback speed for sample reading (e.g., with tabread4~), or even constructing basic oscillators.
